islands.name: Isla ng Salita
islands.description: The villagers here are frozen in silence. Ingay scrambled their words and no one can understand each other. Listen carefully to the words around the unfamiliar ones — context is your compass.
islands.npcDialogueIntro: These villagers are frozen, young sailor. Ingay scrambled their words — turned meaning to noise. Listen to what surrounds the strange words. Context is your compass. It always has been. This is where your journey begins.
islands.npcDialogueSuccess: The villagers breathe again! You unfroze them with your ears. The Vocabulary Shard is yours — first of seven. Sail on, Dagat. The sea is watching.
islands.npcDialogueFail: Ingay's noise still holds them. The clues are in the words nearby — listen again.
islands.ingayDialogue: Frozen in silence, are they? Good. Words without meaning are my favourite kind of noise. Let's see if you can thaw them.
islands.shardDescription: A glowing shard that pulses with the rhythm of a thousand words.

challenges.audioScript: Dagat stepped onto Isla ng Salita for the first time, no shards in her satchel, the salt still wet on her boots. The first frozen villager she found was an old navigator — mouth open, mid-sentence, eyes fixed on a map that no longer moved. Ingay's spell had scrambled his words, but Dagat leaned close and let his fractured voice wash over her. The old navigator unrolled a remarkably elaborate map of the hidden sea. Every current was charted, every reef marked with a red warning stroke, every favorable wind illustrated with delicate arrows. The crew gathered around in silence — none of them had ever seen something so precisely and carefully drawn. She stayed with the word until the sentence around it gave her everything she needed.
challenges.question: What does 'elaborate' most likely mean as used in this passage?

choices.text: Simple and easy to read
choices.label: A

choices.text: Detailed and carefully developed
choices.label: B

choices.text: Enormous in size
choices.label: C

choices.text: Difficult to understand
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The map had every current, reef, and wind carefully marked. These details point to something 'elaborate' — meaning detailed and carefully developed.
challenges.hint: What words describe the map? Those descriptions reveal the word's meaning.

challenges.audioScript: Still on Isla ng Salita, Dagat moved deeper into the frozen village, following the sound of a voice that flickered in and out like a dying lamp. A sailor stood at the dock's edge, frozen mid-story about a voyage that had gone long and hard. Ingay had sealed the words, but Dagat listened through the static. After three weeks at sea with no port in sight, fresh water had become scarce aboard the Baybayin. The cook was rationing half a cup per sailor each day. The captain scanned the horizon from the crow's nest every hour, hoping to spot an island with a freshwater spring. What had once flowed freely from barrels now felt like gold. Dagat felt the weight of the word before she had a name for it.
challenges.question: What does 'scarce' most likely mean as used in the passage?

choices.text: Dirty and unclean
choices.label: A

choices.text: Frozen and icy
choices.label: B

choices.text: Not enough; in short supply
choices.label: C

choices.text: Plentiful and overflowing
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Half a cup per day, rationing, desperate searching — all signal that fresh water was extremely limited. 'Scarce' means not enough; in short supply.
challenges.hint: Why was the captain scanning the horizon? What kind of problem would cause that?

challenges.audioScript: The frozen village went deeper than Dagat had expected. She found a young sailor at the far end of the market square, suspended mid-word, his hands curved as if still holding an invisible rope. He had been telling someone about a sailor named Mando — and the story pressed through Ingay's spell in fragments. Young Mando had failed his navigation test three times. The older sailors laughed and called him 'lost at sea.' But he kept going back to the star charts every night, tracing constellations with his finger until his eyes burned. Two months later, he guided the ship through the treacherous Straits of Alingaso on his own — flawlessly. The captain simply said: 'Those who persevere find their course.' Dagat whispered the last word back to herself, like a compass bearing she intended to keep.
challenges.question: What does 'persevere' mean based on the story?

choices.text: To give up after repeated failure
choices.label: A

choices.text: To continue working hard despite difficulty
choices.label: B

choices.text: To ask others for help immediately
choices.label: C

choices.text: To celebrate before the journey ends
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Mando failed three times but kept studying every night until he succeeded. That persistence — refusing to quit — is exactly what 'persevere' means.
challenges.hint: What did Mando keep doing even after failing? That action mirrors the word's meaning.

challenges.audioScript: Another frozen villager, farther along the same dock — this one a first mate, caught in the posture of grief. His back was to the road, shoulders caved inward, as if Ingay had stopped the world at his worst moment. The story seeping through was quiet and heavy. When the first mate realized the compass had gone overboard in the storm, he walked to the stern alone. He stood with his back to the crew, shoulders curved, staring at the empty sea. He did not speak at dinner. He did not join the card game as he usually did. He just stared at the horizon like a man carrying something no one else could see. Dagat watched his frozen face and felt she understood the word already — before it was spoken.
challenges.question: When the passage describes the first mate as 'dejected,' what does it most likely mean?

choices.text: Excited and full of energy
choices.label: A

choices.text: Sad and deeply discouraged
choices.label: B

choices.text: Confused about the ship's direction
choices.label: C

choices.text: Angry at the storm
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Standing alone at the stern, curved shoulders, refusing dinner and company — all are signs of deep sadness and defeat. That is 'dejected.'
challenges.hint: What emotion fits someone who lost something important and retreated to the stern in silence?

challenges.audioScript: Dagat pressed deeper into Isla ng Salita — past the first dock, past the market stalls, into the quieter middle of the village where Ingay's spell felt thicker. A fisherman stood frozen at the center of the market square, one hand raised mid-gesture, his face set in the expression of someone who had finally made up his mind about something he had resisted for a long time. The story seeped through the interference slowly, like water through cracked timber. Old Isko had not wanted to lend his boat. Everyone in the village knew it. He had crossed his arms and said 'no' twice, and the second time he said it he turned his back on them entirely. But the storm was coming and the crew had no other vessel, and in the end, old Isko's feet walked him back to the dock before his mind had made up its decision. He threw the rope to them without a word. He sat on the dock wall and watched them sail out. When they returned safely, someone asked him why he changed his mind. He didn't answer. He just coiled the rope. Dagat watched his frozen face and understood the word before she found it.
challenges.question: What does 'reluctant' most likely mean as used in the passage?

choices.text: Eager and enthusiastic to help from the start
choices.label: A

choices.text: Unwilling at first but eventually doing something anyway
choices.label: B

choices.text: Angrily refusing to cooperate with anyone
choices.label: C

choices.text: Too confused to make a decision
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Old Isko said no twice and turned his back — but in the end his feet walked him back to the dock. That pattern of unwillingness followed by action is exactly what 'reluctant' means.
challenges.hint: Did Isko want to help? What did he do despite not wanting to? Those two things together describe the word.

challenges.audioScript: Another frozen figure at the edge of the market square — a very young sailor, too new to have calluses yet, caught mid-sentence in some kind of quiet plea to an older officer. The words came through jagged but the picture they painted was clear. Twelve-year-old Tomas had asked the boatswain every morning for two months if he could practice the helm. The boatswain always said 'not yet.' Tomas began arriving an hour before every drill, ropes already coiled, station already set. He stayed after each session to re-clean what had already been cleaned. He wrote the sixteen knots on paper and taped them to the inside of his sea bag and read them before every watch. He never complained. He just tried, quietly and completely, every single day. On the forty-third morning, the boatswain handed him the wheel without a word. Dagat held that picture in her mind — the forty-three mornings — and the word came with it.
challenges.question: What does 'earnest' most likely mean as used in this passage?

choices.text: Naturally talented and skilled without much effort
choices.label: A

choices.text: Quietly hoping someone else will step in to help
choices.label: B

choices.text: Showing deep, sincere effort and dedication
choices.label: C

choices.text: Impatient and eager to skip ahead
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Tomas arrived early, stayed late, studied on his own — every day for forty-three days without being asked. That genuine, wholehearted effort is what 'earnest' means.
challenges.hint: What word describes someone who tries as hard as Tomas did, every day, sincerely and without complaint?

challenges.audioScript: Ingay's hold was getting stronger here — the air near the market district crackled and hissed with interference. A group of sailors were frozen mid-gesture in a tight circle, all of them staring toward the horizon, mouths open. The story crackling through them was urgent and strange. The lookout spotted a peculiar ship drifting at the edge of the fog. It flew no flag. Its sails were patched with mismatched cloth — purple, orange, and deep green. The figurehead at the bow was carved as a crowing rooster, not the usual mermaid. Even the hull was painted in alternating black and white stripes. Every sailor on deck stopped and stared. Dagat pressed her ear toward the sound and held on to the word they kept circling back to.
challenges.question: What does 'peculiar' most likely mean as used in the passage?

choices.text: Very large and powerful
choices.label: A

choices.text: Strange or unusual in a noticeable way
choices.label: B

choices.text: Fast-moving and dangerous
choices.label: C

choices.text: Familiar and ordinary
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: No flag, mismatched sails, a rooster figurehead, striped hull — every detail marks this ship as odd and out of the ordinary. 'Peculiar' means strange or unusual.
challenges.hint: What makes this ship different from every other ship? That difference is what the word describes.

challenges.audioScript: The market of Isla ng Salita was almost completely silent under Ingay's spell — only the hiss of scrambled words and the distant sound of the sea. A frozen dockworker was trying to finish an account of something that had happened to old Kulas, his expression one of bewildered sympathy. Ingay's interference crackled between each word, but Dagat stood still and listened through it. Old Kulas had been to Port Bagyo a dozen times. But that morning the fog was so thick he could barely see his own hands. He wandered the dock for an hour, utterly bewildered. He could not find the harbormaster's office, could not remember which pier the Baybayin was docked at, and was no longer sure whether the sound he heard was a bell or just the wind. The word came through with everything it needed around it.
challenges.question: The passage says Kulas was 'utterly bewildered.' What does this mean?

choices.text: Completely exhausted from walking
choices.label: A

choices.text: Very angry at the fog
choices.label: B

choices.text: Thoroughly confused and disoriented
choices.label: C

choices.text: Happily surprised by the weather
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'Bewildered' means confused. 'Utterly' makes it total — completely disoriented. The three things Kulas could not figure out confirm this.
challenges.hint: What three things did Kulas fail to do? Those failures describe his mental state.

challenges.audioScript: The last corner of Isla ng Salita was quieter than the rest — a hushed kind of cold, as if even Ingay was holding her breath. The Vocabulary Shard was close; Dagat could feel it pulsing somewhere ahead. One more frozen storyteller stood at the village's edge — a veteran sailor, weathered and still, mid-way through a legend she had clearly told a hundred times. Ingay was pushing back hardest here. But Dagat pressed in and listened. Everyone said the eastern passage was impossible in typhoon season. The old captains refused to sail it. But young Captain Hana was tenacious. She mapped the route three times. She studied six months of wind records. She ran two test sails through the outer storm belt. On the day of the crossing, she held the wheel steady for sixteen hours straight — and brought every sailor home. The word rang out like a bell through all the noise.
challenges.question: What does 'tenacious' most likely mean?

choices.text: Naturally skilled from years of training
choices.label: A

choices.text: Stubbornly reckless and dangerous
choices.label: B

choices.text: Determined and persistent no matter the difficulty
choices.label: C

choices.text: Lucky during dangerous crossings
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Three maps, six months of records, two test sails, sixteen hours at the wheel — Hana refused to let difficulty stop her. 'Tenacious' means holding firmly to a goal despite all obstacles.
challenges.hint: Count every extra step Hana took when others refused. That pattern is the word's meaning.

challenges.audioScript: And there he was — the very last frozen villager on Isla ng Salita. Dagat recognized the face before she reached him: old Mang Berto, the island's most legendary navigator, caught mid-story near a lantern that had gone cold. Ingay had frozen him at his most alive moment. Dagat leaned close, close enough to feel the faint warmth still caught in the spell, and his voice broke through in fragments. Old Mang Berto would tell the story every night near the lantern. 'When the Alingawngaw storm hit,' he would say, 'the waves were ferocious — towering walls of black water that swallowed the horizon. Three ships were capsized in a single hour. We tied ourselves to the mast and prayed. I have sailed forty years. I have never seen the sea wear that face again.' Every young sailor listened without a word. Dagat listened the same way now — without a word — until the shard began to glow.
challenges.question: What does 'ferocious' mean as used in the passage?

choices.text: Surprisingly gentle
choices.label: A

choices.text: Ice-cold and freezing
choices.label: B

choices.text: Enormous in height
choices.label: C

choices.text: Violently fierce and powerful
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Towering black waves, three ships capsized, sailors tied to masts — these describe violent, uncontrollable force. That is what 'ferocious' means.
challenges.hint: What did the waves DO to the ships? Those actions define the word.

islands.name: Isla ng Bilis
islands.description: Ingay sped up time on this island. Everyone speaks too fast to follow. Still your mind, breathe slowly, and let the words land where they will.
islands.npcDialogueIntro: The Vocabulary Shard brought you this far — your ears already sharper for it. But Ingay sped up time on Bilis. Everyone speaks too fast to follow. The first shard taught you to hear meaning. Now learn to hear at speed.
islands.npcDialogueSuccess: You matched the current! Two shards now — the Swift Shard shines. Ingay sped up the world and you kept pace. Five islands left, young sailor.
islands.npcDialogueFail: The words slipped past. Ingay wins this round — slow your breath and listen once more.
islands.ingayDialogue: Too fast for you? I made time itself my ally. Every word will blur before it reaches your ears. Enjoy the rush.
islands.shardDescription: A shard that vibrates so fast it hums with invisible energy.

challenges.audioScript: The Vocabulary Shard glowed in Dagat's satchel as the Baybayin dropped anchor at Isla ng Bilis. The moment her boots touched the dock, she felt it — time lurching forward at an unnatural pace, everything spoken at the speed of a panicked heart. Ingay had sped up the island's clock, and the words of every announcement blurred past like water off a tilting hull. Dagat braced herself and caught a fragment from the harbormaster's tower above: Ahoy, crew of the Baybayin! The quartermaster reminds all deckhands that rope-rigging drills will be held this Thursday and Friday — NOT Tuesday as previously posted. Report to the main deck at dawn. Bring your standard kit: gloves, harness line, and climbing hook. Sailors without full kit will not be permitted to drill. Dismissed! She held the correction in her mind like a fixed star.
challenges.question: On which days are the rope-rigging drills now scheduled?

choices.text: Tuesday and Wednesday
choices.label: A

choices.text: Thursday and Friday
choices.label: B

choices.text: Friday and Saturday
choices.label: C

choices.text: Tuesday only
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The announcement corrected the schedule: 'Thursday and Friday — NOT Tuesday.' The correct days are Thursday and Friday.
challenges.hint: Listen for what days are stated AFTER the word 'not.'

challenges.audioScript: Dagat moved through the harbor district of Isla ng Bilis, watching sailors flinch at announcements that came and went in the space of a breath. Everything on this island moved at Ingay's unnatural pace — even the dockmaster's voice streaked past like a ship in full sail. Another notice crackled through the air from the dockmaster's post, and Dagat planted her feet and listened. Port Alingawngaw dockmaster's notice! The supply depot opens weekdays — Monday through Friday — at six bells morning and closes at four bells evening. On weekends — Saturday and Sunday — the depot opens two hours later at eight bells and closes early at two bells afternoon. The depot will be fully sealed during storm warnings. Fair winds! The weekend times were the ones that mattered — she pinned them in place.
challenges.question: What time does the supply depot close on weekends?

choices.text: Four bells in the evening
choices.label: A

choices.text: Two bells in the afternoon
choices.label: B

choices.text: Six bells in the evening
choices.label: C

choices.text: Eight bells in the morning
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'On weekends... closes early at two bells afternoon.' The weekend closing time is two bells in the afternoon.
challenges.hint: Listen specifically for 'weekends' — what closing time follows those words?

challenges.audioScript: Farther into Isla ng Bilis, Ingay's time-acceleration thickened — announcements arriving half-finished, officers mid-sprint between posts. Dagat's ears were sharper now than when she'd landed, but the blur was still fierce. She caught a fleet officer's urgent announcement as the woman ran past, words spilling at an inhuman pace. Attention all officers! The afternoon crew briefing scheduled for three bells today has been cancelled. A broken mainmast on Pier Seven requires all senior riggers to respond immediately. The briefing is rescheduled for tomorrow, same time, same location. Inform your junior deckhands. Move swiftly! Dagat caught the reason before it vanished.
challenges.question: Why was the afternoon crew briefing cancelled?

choices.text: The captain was absent
choices.label: A

choices.text: The meeting room flooded
choices.label: B

choices.text: The harbormaster called for inspection
choices.label: C

choices.text: A broken mainmast required immediate attention
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'A broken mainmast on Pier Seven requires all senior riggers to respond immediately.' That is the stated reason.
challenges.hint: Listen for the word 'requires' — the problem stated there is the reason.

challenges.audioScript: A dispatch runner on Isla ng Bilis came skidding around a corner, speaking so fast she could barely breathe between words — Ingay's time-blur at its most relentless. Dagat stepped directly into the runner's path, steadied her own breathing, and caught the fleet intelligence report mid-delivery. Breaking dispatch from the Coral Triangle lookout post: A scout vessel has identified a previously uncharted island at bearing forty-seven degrees northeast, approximately three days' sail from Port Alingawngaw. The island shows freshwater streams and timber stands. The discovery was reported by helmsman Reyes during last week's scouting run. Fleet commanders are urged to review updated sea charts immediately. The island. Northeast. Three days. She held each detail like a rope in fast water.
challenges.question: What did the scout vessel discover?

choices.text: A new trade route to the west
choices.label: A

choices.text: An uncharted island to the northeast
choices.label: B

choices.text: A sunken wreck near Port Alingawngaw
choices.label: C

choices.text: An enemy fleet at bearing forty-seven
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The dispatch reported: 'a previously uncharted island at bearing forty-seven degrees northeast.' The discovery was a new island.
challenges.hint: What was at bearing forty-seven degrees northeast? That is what was discovered.

challenges.audioScript: Dagat pressed further into the Bilis harbor district, the time-blur now constant — every announcement a blur of sound that she had to slow in her own mind to catch. A fleet assignment officer's voice crackled from a message post ahead, the words overlapping themselves at Ingay's unnatural pace. Attention all crew of the Balisang and the Salabid! Change to pier assignments effective immediately. The Balisang — previously assigned to Pier Four, north side — is now reassigned to Pier Six, south side. Berth space at Pier Four has been taken by an emergency resupply vessel. The Salabid remains at Pier Three as originally posted. All cargo handlers and rigging crew should adjust their positions accordingly. Dock supervisor Mana confirms the change. Fair winds and steady moorings! She caught the number before it vanished.
challenges.question: Where is the Balisang now assigned to dock?

choices.text: Pier Three, north side
choices.label: A

choices.text: Pier Four, south side
choices.label: B

choices.text: Pier Six, south side
choices.label: C

choices.text: Pier Five, north side
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The announcement said the Balisang was 'now reassigned to Pier Six, south side.' Pier Four was taken by another vessel. The new assignment is Pier Six, south side.
challenges.hint: Listen for the word 'now' — the pier stated immediately after it is the new assignment.

challenges.audioScript: A medical supply courier on Isla ng Bilis was reciting an inventory update, speaking at the pace Ingay forced on everything — syllables colliding, pauses removed. Dagat planted her feet and listened for the specific item that mattered. Port Alingawngaw medical depot update! Effective this morning: fresh bandage wraps are now restocked — full supply available. Fever tonic remains out of stock and is not expected until next port call. Surgical thread is available in limited quantities — officers only. Drinking salts are fully restocked. The splint boards ordered three weeks ago have arrived and are available at Counter Two. Report any urgent medical needs to the duty nurse at the Red Anchor station immediately. She had exactly one item on the list to hold: the one still missing.
challenges.question: Which medical supply is still out of stock?

choices.text: Bandage wraps
choices.label: A

choices.text: Surgical thread
choices.label: B

choices.text: Fever tonic
choices.label: C

choices.text: Drinking salts
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'Fever tonic remains out of stock.' All other items mentioned were either restocked or available in some quantity. 'Remains' signals it has not changed.
challenges.hint: Listen for the phrase 'remains out of stock' — which item follows those words?

challenges.audioScript: Dagat was deeper into Isla ng Bilis now, and the time-blur was worse than it had been at the harbor — words piling on top of each other, syllables compressed until they were nearly unrecognizable. Ingay was fighting harder here. A harbormaster's bell notice came screaming through the air, so fast the words nearly tripped over each other, and Dagat braced against a dock post and caught what she could. Good tide, sailors! The harbormaster announces that the cannon inspection originally set for this Wednesday, the fourteenth, has been moved to next Wednesday, the twenty-first. The armory yard remains the same venue. All gunners and powder keepers must attend. Absence without signed medical leave from the ship's surgeon will be recorded. Notify your watch partners. Fair winds! The date had changed. She made herself note what had not.
challenges.question: Why was the cannon inspection rescheduled?

choices.text: The venue was changed
choices.label: A

choices.text: The head gunner was injured
choices.label: B

choices.text: No reason was given — only the date changed
choices.label: C

choices.text: The harbormaster left port
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The announcement said it 'has been moved' — no reason given. The venue stayed the same. When no reason is stated, that IS the answer.
challenges.hint: Did the speaker explain WHY the date changed? If not, that absence of reason IS the answer.

challenges.audioScript: At the supply barge on Isla ng Bilis, the quartermaster was reeling off changes at Ingay's unnatural pace — each figure arriving and disappearing before it could properly land. Dagat stood at the barge's edge and slowed her mind against the rush, letting the numbers come as they would. Attention all crew at the supply barge! Starting at next port call, ration prices have changed. A full salt-fish portion now costs two coppers. Hardtack biscuits are one copper per bundle. Fresh water flasks remain free. The daily hot stew — previously three coppers — has been reduced to two coppers. All prices effective beginning Monday. The word 'reduced' was the anchor she needed.
challenges.question: How much does the daily hot stew cost under the new prices?

choices.text: Three coppers
choices.label: A

choices.text: Four coppers
choices.label: B

choices.text: One copper
choices.label: C

choices.text: Two coppers
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The stew 'has been reduced to two coppers.' The new price is two coppers — three was the old price.
challenges.hint: Listen for 'reduced to' — the number right after it is the new price.

challenges.audioScript: The Swift Shard was close — Dagat could feel it the way she had felt the first shard, a warmth pulling at the edge of the air. But Ingay was pouring everything into this island now, and the fleet commander's announcement was nearly unintelligible, the words compressed to a blur of urgent sound. Dagat closed her eyes, slowed her breath, and listened with everything she had. Fast news from the fleet commander! The joint fleet maneuvers originally planned for this Friday have been postponed to the following Friday. All ship positions and formations remain the same. The drill route is unchanged. Participation is required for all captains and first mates. No late arrivals will be excused. Signal your fleet neighbors. Dismissed! She caught the most important absence in the whole announcement: the reason no one gave.
challenges.question: Why were the joint fleet maneuvers postponed?

choices.text: The fleet formation changed
choices.label: A

choices.text: Too many ships were unavailable
choices.label: B

choices.text: No reason was given — only the date changed
choices.label: C

choices.text: The commander was called away
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The announcement only said the maneuvers 'have been postponed' — no reason given. Route and formations stayed the same.
challenges.hint: Did the speaker say WHY the date moved? If nothing is stated, the answer is 'no reason given.'

challenges.audioScript: The final challenge on Isla ng Bilis — and this was where Ingay almost won. The maritime report flew past in a blur so complete that even the numbers seemed to vibrate. Dagat thought of Captain Hana, whose fleet had sailed the Eastern Passage through sixteen hours of storm, and she slowed her mind the same way she imagined Hana steadying the wheel. Maritime dispatch: A new survey by the Coral Sea Navigation Council confirms that crews sailing the Eastern Passage with fewer than six hours of night watch rest perform at three times the error rate of crews resting eight or more hours. The survey tracked two hundred vessels over two full sailing seasons. Council officials recommend all fleet captains mandate minimum rest schedules before entering the passage. The number that mattered sat quiet at the center of the blur — and she caught it.
challenges.question: How many hours of rest does the survey associate with better performance?

choices.text: At least five hours
choices.label: A

choices.text: At least six hours
choices.label: B

choices.text: Eight or more hours
choices.label: C

choices.text: Between nine and ten hours
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The survey compared under-6-hours crews to those resting 'eight or more hours.' The better-performing group rested eight or more hours.
challenges.hint: Two groups are compared. Which had fewer errors? How many hours did they rest?

islands.name: Isla ng Diwa
islands.description: Ingay covered this island in thick fog. The main idea is buried beneath layers of detail and misdirection. Find what the speaker keeps returning to — that is the heart of it.
islands.npcDialogueIntro: Two shards already in your satchel. Ingay's answer to your speed is fog — she buried the main idea under layers of detail on Isla ng Diwa. Ask yourself: what point does the speaker return to most? That thread is your lantern.
islands.npcDialogueSuccess: The fog lifts! Three shards now — the Clarity Shard is yours. You are halfway through the Listening Sea, Dagat. Halfway home.
islands.npcDialogueFail: Ingay's fog won this time. Find what connects all the details — that thread is the main idea.
islands.ingayDialogue: I wrapped this island in fog so thick, not even the main idea can breathe. Find it — if you can see through me.
islands.shardDescription: A shard as clear as still water on a windless morning.

challenges.audioScript: Two shards in her satchel now, and Isla ng Diwa swallowed Dagat the moment she stepped ashore — the fog was total, white and close, pressing against her skin like damp cloth. Somewhere ahead in the fog, a voice was speaking. She recognized the cadence before she understood the words: it was the old navigator she'd met frozen on Isla ng Salita, somehow here ahead of her, giving a speech to an invisible audience. She pushed through the mist and listened for the thread. Every experienced sailor knows what the young ones forget: the most dangerous thing on a ship is not the storm or the reef — it is a tired navigator. Studies from the Maritime Safety Guild show that navigators who sleep fewer than six hours make three times as many course errors. Your charts, your compass, your eyes — none of them work if the brain behind them is exhausted. Rest is not laziness. It is the foundation of safe sailing. Dagat found the main point before the fog swallowed the voice again.
challenges.question: What is the main idea of this passage?

choices.text: Why navigators should study star charts more
choices.label: A

choices.text: The importance of rest for safe navigation
choices.label: B

choices.text: How to prepare for a storm at sea
choices.label: C

choices.text: Tips for choosing a reliable compass
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The passage mentions charts and compasses briefly, but the entire argument centers on sleep and its consequences for navigation. Rest for safe sailing is the main point.
challenges.hint: What topic does the speaker keep returning to? That is the main idea.

challenges.audioScript: The fog deepened further into Isla ng Diwa, and the voice Dagat caught now belonged to a ship's captain — lecturing her crew somewhere in the white, the sound dampened and muffled by Ingay's mist. Details arrived first: names of storms, dates, geography. The main idea kept slipping beneath them. Dagat reached through the layers and followed the thread. Most sailors fear storms — and rightly so. But storms have shaped these islands as much as any calm current. The great typhoon of 1714 carved the natural harbor at Port Salita. The seasonal squalls push nutrient-rich water up from the depths, feeding the fish that feed the fleet. The Eastern Passage itself — the most direct trade route in the region — was discovered by a crew blown off course in a storm. Storms do not only destroy. Sometimes, they reveal. She let the final line land like an anchor.
challenges.question: What is the main point of this passage?

choices.text: Typhoons only cause destruction and should be avoided
choices.label: A

choices.text: Storms have played an important and sometimes positive role in shaping the sea world
choices.label: B

choices.text: The Eastern Passage is the safest trade route
choices.label: C

choices.text: Sailors should welcome every storm as an opportunity
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Each example — the harbor, the fish, the trade route — shows a constructive outcome from storms. Their complex, sometimes beneficial role is the main point.
challenges.hint: What connects all three examples the speaker gives? That connection is the main idea.

challenges.audioScript: Still in the fog, Dagat found that the voice now belonged to the Baybayin's own captain — addressing her own crew, somewhere nearby in the white. Ingay's fog buried the point under practical details: rope counts, canvas weights, timber tallies. But Dagat listened past the numbers for what connected them all. Every season, fleets across the Listening Sea throw away enough rope, canvas, and timber to outfit five new vessels. Today I want to share three habits for the Baybayin's crew. First: before requesting new supplies, inspect what you already have. Second: repair before replacing — a sailor who can splice rope is worth twice his weight in gold. Third: repurpose what you cannot repair. Old sail canvas makes excellent cargo covers. Small changes in how we manage ship's materials add up to a large difference. Three habits. One goal. She had it.
challenges.question: What is the passage mainly about?

choices.text: How to reduce supply waste on a ship
choices.label: A

choices.text: The shortage of timber in the fleet
choices.label: B

choices.text: Different ways to repair rope and canvas
choices.label: C

choices.text: Why new supplies are expensive at port
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: All three tips — inspect, repair, repurpose — connect to one goal: reducing waste aboard ship. That is the main idea.
challenges.hint: What action does the speaker want the crew to take? That goal is the main idea.

challenges.audioScript: Deeper into the fog, two voices rose from opposite directions — a debate between sailors, their words wrapping around each other as Ingay's mist tangled the sound. One said one thing, one said another, and the argument seemed to go on and on. But then one voice rose above it — clearer, steadier, aimed at the heart of the matter. Dagat listened to it alone. Some captains say new navigation tools have ruined the old art of reading stars and currents. Others say they are the future of the fleet. I say both sides are missing the point. A sextant, a compass, a new chart — these are tools. The question is not whether to use them, but whether you understand the sea well enough to know when they are right and when they are wrong. Instruments used well make better sailors. That was the point. Not which side — what lay between them.
challenges.question: What is the speaker's main argument?

choices.text: Old navigation methods should replace modern instruments
choices.label: A

choices.text: Modern tools have made sailors worse at their craft
choices.label: B

choices.text: Good instruments, used with understanding, make better sailors
choices.label: C

choices.text: Sailors should only rely on star charts
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The speaker acknowledges the debate but concludes: 'Instruments used well make better sailors.' That final statement is the main argument.
challenges.hint: What does the speaker say at the very end? The conclusion usually holds the main argument.

challenges.audioScript: Still in the fog of Isla ng Diwa — thicker now at Pin 3, pressing in from all sides. A voice rose from somewhere to Dagat's left, unhurried and tired, full of complaints she had to push past to find what the speaker really wanted. A dock officer, it sounded like, addressing a restless crew. The morning gale delayed us by six hours. The supply barge from the northern islands still hasn't arrived. Three of our best riggers are down with sea fever. The charts we requested from Port Salita haven't been corrected yet. I know you're frustrated. I know the dock is crowded and the timeline has slipped. But I need you all to do one thing: wait. Hold your stations. Let the situation resolve itself. These things always do. The fog always lifts. The barge always comes. Our job right now is to stay steady and trust the process. Dagat found it — underneath every complaint, just one thing the officer wanted from the crew.
challenges.question: What is the main point of the officer's speech?

choices.text: To explain why the supply barge is running late
choices.label: A

choices.text: To report the full list of problems they are facing
choices.label: B

choices.text: To urge the crew to stay patient through difficult delays
choices.label: C

choices.text: To announce that three riggers are too sick to work
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The officer lists delays but keeps returning to one message: wait, hold steady, trust the process. The complaints are background details. Patience is the point.
challenges.hint: What does the officer keep asking the crew to do? That repeated instruction is the main point.

challenges.audioScript: Deeper into the fog, a captain's voice reached Dagat — measured and deliberate, listing many things but clearly building toward one. Dagat breathed slowly and followed the thread from detail to detail until she found where it led. We have checked every hatch and sealed the ones showing wear. The bilge pumps have been tested — all working. Each sailor has been assigned their storm station. The safety ropes have been doubled along the main deck. The cook has prepared cold provisions in sealed tins in case the galley fire must be put out. We have done what we can with the time we have. There is only one thing that matters now: every decision we make from this point forward must put the safety of this crew first. Nothing else. No schedule, no cargo, no reputation — only the crew. Dagat followed it from beginning to end and saw exactly where it landed.
challenges.question: What is the main message of the captain's speech?

choices.text: To give a detailed checklist of all the storm preparations
choices.label: A

choices.text: To warn the crew that the galley fire might go out
choices.label: B

choices.text: To emphasize that the crew's safety is the top priority
choices.label: C

choices.text: To explain that schedules and cargo still matter
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The captain lists preparations, but the speech ends with one point: everything must put 'the safety of this crew first.' Every detail serves that conclusion.
challenges.hint: What is the very last thing the captain says? The final statement usually carries the main message.

challenges.audioScript: Pin 4 — the fog was at its thickest here, pressing in from all sides, Ingay working hardest to bury every clear thought under a flood of detail. Dagat could hear a port official somewhere ahead, speaking in long, careful sentences that piled fact upon fact. She slowed herself, breathed, and listened for the one thing the official kept returning to. Three years ago, a small group of island women began growing vegetables in an unused corner of Port Salita's eastern dock. What started as a side project became something larger. Today, the garden supplies fresh provisions to twelve vessels each week — reducing scurvy cases aboard ship by forty percent. Sailors from other ports have begun requesting seeds to bring home. What was once a corner of weeds has become one of the port's most valuable assets. The transformation. Start to finish. That was what the speech was really about.
challenges.question: What is the main idea of this passage?

choices.text: A failed dock project that was eventually cleared away
choices.label: A

choices.text: A small garden that grew into a vital port resource
choices.label: B

choices.text: How to prevent scurvy on long sea voyages
choices.label: C

choices.text: The importance of regular dock maintenance
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The passage traces the garden from a side project to a resource other ports want to replicate. Its growth and impact is the main idea.
challenges.hint: Follow the story from start to finish. What did the garden become? That transformation is the main idea.

challenges.audioScript: Still deep in the fog of Isla ng Diwa, a harbormaster's voice carried from somewhere near the water — a long historical account of Port Dagat's waterway, told with careful dates and species names and decade-by-decade statistics. Ingay's fog kept burying the most important point under the flood of facts. Dagat let the details wash past and listened only for the thread that connected them. The channel into Port Dagat once ran dark with bilge waste and dye from the dockside cloth works. Fish had vanished. Fishermen abandoned their boats. Then, in the 1990s, a gradual cleanup effort began. Waste controls were enforced. Industrial dumping was banned. Today, over thirty species of fish have returned to the channel. Sea birds nest along the banks once more. The channel is not fully restored — but it breathes again. Recovery — slow, real, incomplete — was the heart of it.
challenges.question: What is the main point of this passage?

choices.text: Industrial activity always destroys waterways permanently
choices.label: A

choices.text: The Port Dagat channel has been fully restored to its original state
choices.label: B

choices.text: The Port Dagat channel is slowly recovering after years of cleanup
choices.label: C

choices.text: Fish cannot survive in ports used by trading vessels
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The passage shows decline then recovery. 'Not fully restored — but breathing again' signals gradual, real but incomplete recovery. Option B overstates it.
challenges.hint: Did the passage say 'fully restored'? Listen for 'not' — it changes the main idea.

challenges.audioScript: The Clarity Shard was close — Dagat could feel it humming somewhere beyond the thickest wall of fog she had faced yet. A fleet commander's voice reached her from the far side, each word muffled and pressed flat by Ingay's mist. She pushed toward the sound and dragged the meaning out word by word, refusing to let the fog win. Surveys show that sailors who choose their voyages freely — picking routes they genuinely want to sail — perform better, stay healthier, and remain with their fleet longer. Yet many young sailors drift to whatever ship will take them. The reasons vary: desperation for work, no knowledge of options, or simply not believing they deserve a choice. The answer is not to lecture sailors about their worth. It is to find them the voyage they cannot wait to begin. The final line landed clear as sunlight through a break in the clouds.
challenges.question: What is the speaker's main argument?

choices.text: Sailors are lazy and need more discipline
choices.label: A

choices.text: Young sailors should take any available route
choices.label: B

choices.text: Helping sailors find voyages they love leads to better outcomes
choices.label: C

choices.text: Paying sailors more is the key to loyalty
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The speaker ends with: 'find them the voyage they cannot wait to begin.' This is the argument.
challenges.hint: What does the speaker say at the very end? That is usually where the main argument lands.

challenges.audioScript: The last speech before the fog broke — and it was the hardest one yet. Dagat reached toward a Maritime Scholars' voice through what felt like a wall of white wool, the sound reduced to a near-whisper by Ingay's mist. She extended every part of her attention toward it, and the words came through slowly, one layer at a time. Research by the Maritime Scholars' Guild shows that sailors who train in traditional sea chants and oral navigation songs for two or more years develop sharper memory for sequences, better distance estimation, and stronger focus during long watches. These effects persist for the rest of their careers. Sea chant training is not entertainment — it is a core investment in how a navigator's mind works. Fleets that have cut chant training to save time may be cutting the very thing that sharpens everything else. The Clarity Shard pulsed bright the moment she had it.
challenges.question: What is the speaker's main point?

choices.text: Sea chant training should be optional for experienced sailors
choices.label: A

choices.text: Sailors who know chants perform better at port festivals
choices.label: B

choices.text: Traditional sea chant training meaningfully supports a navigator's skills
choices.label: C

choices.text: Research on sea chants and memory is still inconclusive
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The speaker presents evidence and concludes chant training is 'a core investment in how a navigator's mind works.' Option C captures this without overstating.
challenges.hint: What does the speaker want fleet commanders to stop cutting? That thing is the main point.

islands.name: Isla ng Damdamin
islands.description: Ingay drained all emotion from this island. Everyone speaks in the same flat voice. But feelings hide in pauses, in speed, in word choice — listen for the heart, not just the words.
islands.npcDialogueIntro: Three shards. Ingay tried scrambled words, blinding speed, and smothering fog — and you answered every one. Now she drains emotion. Words on this island are flat and colorless. But feelings hide in pauses, in pitch, in the spaces between syllables. Listen for them.
islands.npcDialogueSuccess: You heard the heart behind the voice! The Empathy Shard glows warm in your hands — four shards now. Ingay's tricks grow crueler. And your ears grow sharper.
islands.npcDialogueFail: The feeling was there. Listen for HOW the speaker says it, not just WHAT they say.
islands.ingayDialogue: I drained every feeling from their voices. Flat. Colourless. Emotionless. Let's see you find the heart in nothing.
islands.shardDescription: A warm shard that glows red when you hold it close.

challenges.audioScript: Three shards now — and Isla ng Damdamin was unlike anything Dagat had faced before. The island wasn't loud or fast or foggy. It was simply flat. Every voice she heard arrived in the same grey, colorless register, as if Ingay had drained every drop of feeling from the air. A navigator's voice reached her first — the words were all there, technically correct, but something essential was missing. Dagat forced herself to listen past the surface. I've been charting this route for three weeks and I'm still not sure it's right. What if we hit the reef at low tide? What if the current shifts? I ran the numbers ten times last night and I still feel like I'm missing something. I hope this crossing works. I really do. But every time I look at the chart, I find another line I want to redraw. I just — I don't know. I really don't know. The tone was buried — but the words, she realized, carried it anyway.
challenges.question: What is the overall tone of the speaker?

choices.text: Angry and bitter toward the crew
choices.label: A

choices.text: Joyful and confident about the crossing
choices.label: B

choices.text: Worried and deeply uncertain
choices.label: C

choices.text: Proud of the route they have planned
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'I'm not sure,' 'what if,' 'I don't know' — these expressions of self-doubt and anxiety signal a worried, uncertain tone.
challenges.hint: Listen to HOW the speaker says things. 'What if' and 'I don't know' are key emotional signals.

challenges.audioScript: Another voice on Isla ng Damdamin — a sailor recounting a rough morning — arrived in the same flat, colorless register that Ingay's drain produced. The words described something that had clearly started badly and turned around, but the feeling of that turn was invisible. Dagat traced the arc of the story anyway, feeling for where it shifted. The morning started terribly. The anchor chain jammed, the bilge pump failed, and rain soaked my chart bag before I could cover it. I thought the whole day was ruined. But then my crewmate brought me dried fish and a warm blanket without saying a word, and another sailor stayed up to help me redraw the soaked charts. By the time we set sail, I realized — it wasn't such a bad day after all. Some things fix themselves, if you let your crew help. She felt the shift from bad to better — even through the drain.
challenges.question: What can you infer about how the speaker feels at the END?

choices.text: Relieved that the day turned out better than expected
choices.label: A

choices.text: Still frustrated about the anchor and bilge
choices.label: B

choices.text: Completely indifferent about the crew's help
choices.label: C

choices.text: Deeply regretful about the ruined charts
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The day started badly but ended with crew kindness and gratitude. 'Wasn't such a bad day after all' signals a shift from frustration to relief.
challenges.hint: Compare how the speaker felt at the START vs the END. Did the mood change?

challenges.audioScript: At the port's edge on Isla ng Damdamin, a festival was frozen mid-celebration — streamers limp, drums unbeaten, the port captain mid-speech before a crowd that could no longer feel what she was saying. Under Ingay's full emotional drain, the pride in her voice was invisible. Every grand word arrived flat. Dagat stood at the back of the silent crowd and listened past the flatness for the feeling the captain was trying to make the crowd feel. This port has been running for one hundred and forty years. Captains come back from distant seas — from trading routes far beyond the horizon — just to dock here during the Alingawngaw Festival. This is not just a celebration. This is proof of who we are. Our grandfathers built these piers. Our children will sail from them. We carry their courage in our hands when we haul the rigging. We honor them every time we leave and every time we return. This harbor is ours, and we are proud. The pride was there. She found it in the words themselves.
challenges.question: What feeling does the speaker most want to create in the listener?

choices.text: Guilt for abandoning the port in the past
choices.label: A

choices.text: Pride in the port's heritage and community
choices.label: B

choices.text: Fear about the port's future
choices.label: C

choices.text: Confusion about the port's history
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Uplifting language about heritage, ancestors, and community — 'proof of who we are,' 'our grandfathers,' 'we are proud' — all create a sense of pride in belonging.
challenges.hint: What emotion fits when someone speaks admiringly about your community's history and traditions?

challenges.audioScript: A ship's log entry read aloud — a captain's quiet account of a moment of decision at sea. Under Ingay's drain, even this exchange of quiet strength sounded empty, mechanical, as if the words were just words and nothing more. Dagat closed her eyes and reached for the attitude beneath them. I told the first mate to change the heading. He said it was too late to turn. I said we had no choice. He looked at me for a long time, then nodded slowly. 'Right,' he said. 'If that's how the wind blows — it blows.' He didn't argue after that. He just took the wheel, made the correction, and said nothing more. No complaints, no second-guessing. Just quiet acceptance of the sea's decision. The posture of the first mate — that nod, that single phrase — told her everything.
challenges.question: What does 'if that's how the wind blows — it blows' reveal about the first mate's attitude?

choices.text: Excitement about the new heading
choices.label: A

choices.text: Quiet acceptance of something he cannot change
choices.label: B

choices.text: Refusal to understand the problem
choices.label: C

choices.text: Eagerness to find a different route
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'It blows' expresses acceptance of an unchangeable reality. He took the wheel and said nothing more — confirming quiet acceptance.
challenges.hint: What does this phrase mean in a sailor's world? What attitude does accepting the wind express?

challenges.audioScript: A sailor's report on Isla ng Damdamin — flat as everything else here, every trace of emotion stripped by Ingay's drain. But Dagat had learned to read the shape of words even without their feeling. This one described a hard moment at the dock. I tried to bring the Baybayin in at the south berth during the squall. The wind shifted twice while I was on approach. The first attempt, I had to abort when the swell pushed us sideways. Second attempt, we got within twenty meters and lost the line. I radioed the harbormaster to let him know we'd try again at first light. Got some rest. Went back. Third attempt worked. We were six hours behind schedule. But we docked. Dagat found the tone beneath the flat report — not complaint, not sorrow, not defeat. Something quieter and harder than any of those.
challenges.question: What is the overall tone of the speaker's report?

choices.text: Defeated and bitter about the failed attempts
choices.label: A

choices.text: Calm and determined despite multiple setbacks
choices.label: B

choices.text: Frustrated and angry at the harbormaster
choices.label: C

choices.text: Confused about why the attempts failed
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: No blame, no self-pity — just 'tried again, worked.' The speaker reports difficulty factually and moves directly to the outcome. That is calm determination.
challenges.hint: How does the speaker respond to each failed attempt? Their reaction to failure reveals their tone.

challenges.audioScript: A veteran navigator was giving directions to a young sailor on Isla ng Damdamin — her voice completely stripped of warmth by Ingay's drain, arriving as flat instruction, technical and dry. Dagat stood nearby and listened for what was hidden underneath. The current here pulls northeast at six knots when the tide shifts. You'll feel it in the wheel before you see it in the water. Correct early — don't wait until the bow is already moving. When you start feeling that pull, put in the adjustment now. Most sailors your age overcorrect and end up worse than they started. You don't do that. You've got a quiet hand. I've seen it. That is going to serve you a very long time at sea. The words were mostly technical — but one thing was not technical at all. Dagat heard what the flatness was trying to hide.
challenges.question: Which phrase reveals that the navigator has a positive view of the young sailor?

choices.text: "The current here pulls northeast at six knots"
choices.label: A

choices.text: "You'll feel it in the wheel before you see it in the water"
choices.label: B

choices.text: "You've got a quiet hand. I've seen it."
choices.label: C

choices.text: "Most sailors your age overcorrect"
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'You've got a quiet hand. I've seen it.' is personal observation and quiet praise. The rest is technical instruction about currents. Only that phrase reveals genuine positive regard.
challenges.hint: Which line is about the sailor as a person, not about a sailing technique? That line holds the feeling.

challenges.audioScript: Dagat found a training dock on Isla ng Damdamin where a captain was addressing a young sailor. Under Ingay's full emotional drain, the captain's words sounded almost cold — each sentence arriving without warmth, without the firmness that Dagat could tell was supposed to be there. She stood at the edge of the dock and listened past the flatness, reaching for the warmth she knew must exist somewhere behind the words. You dropped the anchor too late. I know. But I want you to remember something: the sailor who gets it right every time is not the one I am looking for. I am looking for the sailor who gets it wrong, goes below, figures out why, and comes back to the wheel before anyone else is awake. That is the sailor who goes somewhere. You dropped it too late. Good. Now show me what you do with that. There it was — encouragement, pressed tight under the surface, firm and alive.
challenges.question: What is the overall tone of the speaker?

choices.text: Critical and disappointed in the sailor
choices.label: A

choices.text: Encouraging and challenging in a firm way
choices.label: B

choices.text: Indifferent and uncaring
choices.label: C

choices.text: Proud and celebratory
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The captain acknowledges the mistake but immediately reframes it as opportunity. Firm — 'good, now show me' — but clearly motivating.
challenges.hint: Is the speaker trying to discourage or motivate? What does the last sentence reveal?

challenges.audioScript: An awards ceremony on Isla ng Damdamin — a fleet captain speaking before the assembled crew, the gratitude in her voice stripped to nothing by Ingay's drain. Each image she offered arrived like a flat report rather than a tribute. Dagat stood among the motionless sailors and listened for the weight that should have been there. When I look at all of you today, I don't see sailors. I see years of early mornings at the dock, of families waving from the shore, of riggers who stayed through the night to patch a sail before dawn. I see navigators who recalculated the course three times and still got us home. I see a crew who has carried each other through storms no one should have to sail through. You didn't complete this voyage alone. Every person in this harbor completed it with you. The gratitude was embedded in every image — she just had to be willing to feel it.
challenges.question: What emotion is the speaker most trying to create?

choices.text: Regret for past failures
choices.label: A

choices.text: Gratitude and a sense of shared achievement
choices.label: B

choices.text: Pride in individual skill
choices.label: C

choices.text: Nostalgia for earlier voyages
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Every image — early mornings, families, the navigator's extra work — points to shared effort. 'You didn't complete this voyage alone' creates collective gratitude.
challenges.hint: What does 'you didn't complete this voyage alone' mean? What emotion does shared achievement create?

challenges.audioScript: The Empathy Shard was close — Dagat could feel it, a warmth at the edge of the cold drain. Ingay's power over this island was total now: even tenderness arrived without texture, even hope sounded like a ledger entry. Dagat found a letter, handwritten, that someone had been reading aloud when the drain hit them. She took it in her own hands and read the words aloud herself, trying to find the warmth beneath the emotionless voice. The letter was from a navigator named Marina — someone who sailed far, whose warmth crossed great distances through these few written lines. I am writing this from a port you may never visit. I don't know what seas you are sailing now, or what storms you are carrying. But I want you to know that the sailor who wrote these words believed in you — not the perfect crossing, not the flawless charts, not the unblemished logbook. You. The one who gets back up when the wave knocks them down. I left a little of my courage here between these lines. Take as much as you need. Dagat felt it — warmth, quiet and sure, between every line.
challenges.question: What is the tone of this passage?

choices.text: Formal and instructional
choices.label: A

choices.text: Sad and full of regret
choices.label: B

choices.text: Warm, personal, and quietly hopeful
choices.label: C

choices.text: Urgent and alarming
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The language is intimate — 'I am writing to you,' 'I want you to know.' The message is trust and courage. 'I left a little of my courage here' is tender and quiet.
challenges.hint: Imagine receiving this message from an old sailor. How would it make you feel? That feeling matches the tone.

challenges.audioScript: The final challenge of Isla ng Damdamin — and Ingay's drain reached its peak here, stripping even this moment of every feeling it deserved. The story Dagat heard was a farewell — sailors at the end of a long voyage, standing on the dock together — and it should have been bittersweet and full. Under the drain, it sounded matter-of-fact. Dagat closed her eyes and reached for the feeling beneath the flat words, refusing to let Ingay take it. The dock felt different the morning the voyage ended. No one was in a hurry to disembark. Sailors who had barely spoken for months were exchanging port addresses, helping each other with their sea bags. Someone chalked on the hull: 'We were here.' The captain had it washed off before anyone could stop her, then turned around and said, 'You'll always be here.' No one really knew what to say after that. So they just stood on the dock together, for a while. There — in the silence, in the standing together — was everything Ingay had tried to take.
challenges.question: What feeling does the passage create most powerfully?

choices.text: Relief that the voyage is finally over
choices.label: A

choices.text: Excitement about the next voyage
choices.label: B

choices.text: Bittersweet emotion at the end of something meaningful
choices.label: C

choices.text: Sadness mixed with anger
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Sailors connecting before parting, the chalked hull, the captain's quiet response — all create a tender, bittersweet feeling. Sad it's ending, meaningful enough to remember.
challenges.hint: What did the crew do at the very end? Standing together in silence usually signals what kind of feeling?

islands.name: Isla ng Tanong
islands.description: Ingay scattered the island's records — names, dates, numbers, all jumbled. Listen for the exact detail and hold it in your mind. The facts are there. You just have to catch them.
islands.npcDialogueIntro: Four shards. Ingay scattered the island's records — names, dates, numbers, all jumbled and lost. You have learned to hear meaning, speed, main ideas, and feeling. Now hold a single precise fact like a compass needle in your mind. Do not let it slip.
islands.npcDialogueSuccess: You caught the exact detail Ingay tried to scatter! Five shards, young sailor. One island of preparation before the last.
islands.npcDialogueFail: Ingay hid the detail well. The specific fact is always spoken — it waits for ears patient enough to catch it.
islands.ingayDialogue: Names. Dates. Numbers. I scattered every last one. You'll reach for a fact and find only static.
islands.shardDescription: A sharp-edged shard that reflects light at exact, perfect angles.

challenges.audioScript: Four shards gleamed in Dagat's satchel as she stepped onto Isla ng Tanong. The island's air felt scattered — names darting past like startled fish, numbers floating up and dissolving before they could be caught. Ingay had jumbled everything: records, registries, facts, dates. A fleet health announcement drifted toward Dagat, and she steadied herself against a dock post and listened for the one precise number that mattered. Good tide, young crew. I'm Officer Catalan from the Fleet Health Office. According to the 1824 Maritime Sailors' Survey, one in three crew members on long voyages is considered dangerously undernourished by the halfway point. Iron deficiency affects approximately forty-two percent of sailors on journeys of six months or more. The Fleet Council recommends iron-rich provisions — especially dried legumes — at least four times per week. Start with a cup of monggo stew every other day. Two numbers, two different facts. She caught the right one.
challenges.question: What percentage of sailors on long voyages are affected by iron deficiency?

choices.text: One in three
choices.label: A

choices.text: Thirty percent
choices.label: B

choices.text: Forty-two percent
choices.label: C

choices.text: Fifty percent
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'Iron deficiency affects approximately forty-two percent.' Note: 'one in three' refers to undernourishment — a different statistic.
challenges.hint: The speaker gives two separate numbers. Listen for which one is tied specifically to 'iron deficiency.'

challenges.audioScript: The registry clerk on Isla ng Tanong was reading numbers — totals, subtotals, route breakdowns, season comparisons — while Ingay's scatter sent several wrong figures floating in the air around each correct one. Dagat planted herself in front of the clerk and listened for the specific number that answered the specific question she had been given. Good tide, fleet officers. This is the official crew registry for the current sailing season. Total registered crew across all vessels stands at one thousand two hundred forty-three sailors — six hundred eighty-two on merchant routes and five hundred sixty-one on patrol routes. Compared to last season's total of one thousand one hundred ninety-eight, we have seen an increase of forty-five sailors. Growth is projected to continue. Thank you. The increase. Not the total. The increase. She had it.
challenges.question: By how many sailors did the fleet registry increase compared to last season?

choices.text: Forty-two sailors
choices.label: A

choices.text: Forty-five sailors
choices.label: B

choices.text: One thousand two hundred forty-three sailors
choices.label: C

choices.text: Six hundred eighty-two sailors
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'Compared to last season, we have seen an increase of forty-five sailors.' The increase is forty-five — not the total count.
challenges.hint: Listen for the word 'increase' — what number follows directly after it?

challenges.audioScript: A competition announcement on Isla ng Tanong — and Ingay's scatter meant names were flying everywhere, third place tangling with first, wrong ships attached to wrong sailors. Dagat heard a familiar name mentioned — the Baybayin — and leaned in sharply. Good tide! I am pleased to announce the results of the Fleet Navigation Challenge. We received forty-three entries this season — our highest number ever. Third place goes to helmsman Lorenzo Bautista of the Makisig. Second place goes to navigator Reina Flores of the Hamon. And first prize — and I am proud to say this — goes to junior navigator Ana Reyes of the Baybayin. Congratulations to all competitors! Her ship. First prize. She had to make sure she caught the right name and the right place — the scatter was designed to confuse exactly this.
challenges.question: Who won the Fleet Navigation Challenge?

choices.text: Maria Santos
choices.label: A

choices.text: Jose Rivera
choices.label: B

choices.text: Ana Reyes
choices.label: C

choices.text: Lorenzo Bautista
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'First prize goes to junior navigator Ana Reyes of the Baybayin.' The winner is Ana Reyes. Lorenzo Bautista took third — not first.
challenges.hint: Names are announced in order — third, second, first. Listen for the name that follows 'first prize.'

challenges.audioScript: The fleet surgeon on Isla ng Tanong was addressing a crowd gathered near the medical dock, and Ingay's scatter kept snatching the exact recommendation away before it could land cleanly. Wrong numbers drifted in at the edges — four flasks, six flasks, ten flasks — designed to confuse. Dagat listened past the interference for the one number the surgeon had actually said. Good tide. I'm the fleet surgeon, and I want to talk about hydration on long voyages. Many of you are not drinking enough water. At sea, the body loses water faster — through sweat, sun, and wind. The recommendation for all active crew: at least eight flasks of fresh water every day. Not brine water, not grog — fresh water. Your navigation depends on a clear mind. A clear mind depends on water. Eight. She held it like a compass needle pointed true north.
challenges.question: How many flasks of fresh water per day does the fleet surgeon recommend?

choices.text: Four flasks
choices.label: A

choices.text: Six flasks
choices.label: B

choices.text: Eight flasks
choices.label: C

choices.text: Ten flasks
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'The recommendation for all active crew: at least eight flasks of fresh water every day.' The recommendation is eight flasks.
challenges.hint: Medical advice often uses 'at least' before a number. Listen for those words.

challenges.audioScript: Four shards gleamed in Dagat's satchel as she pushed deeper into Isla ng Tanong — Ingay's scatter at its most relentless here, facts darting away mid-sentence. A port supply captain's voice cut through the noise with an update, and Dagat caught it with everything she had. Attention fleet crew — supply update for the week's long sail. We are leaving port with forty-two barrels of fresh water. That sounds like a lot. With a full crew of thirty-eight sailors drinking and cooking, we will have enough for exactly fourteen days at normal rations. Our scheduled resupply stop is at the Coral Station waypoint on the tenth day. I want every quartermaster to confirm their section's daily water log by this evening. If anything seems off, report it to me before we cast off. Fair winds and tight barrels. Dagat locked the two numbers that mattered most.
challenges.question: How many barrels of fresh water are they leaving port with?

choices.text: Thirty-eight
choices.label: A

choices.text: Ten
choices.label: B

choices.text: Fourteen
choices.label: C

choices.text: Forty-two
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'We are leaving port with forty-two barrels.' Thirty-eight is the crew count, fourteen is how long the water lasts, and ten is the resupply day — all different numbers with different meanings.
challenges.hint: Listen for the number that comes right after the words 'leaving port with.' That is the barrel count.

challenges.audioScript: A maritime training announcement crackled through Isla ng Tanong — Ingay's scatter working hardest on the specific details, the facts darting away before Dagat could anchor them. She stopped, steadied herself, and caught the announcement as it reached a key correction. Attention all junior crew! The anchor-handling drill scheduled for this afternoon will be held at Dock Fourteen — not Dock Eleven as originally posted. Please note: the drill begins at two bells past noon, not at noon itself. Participants must bring their standard rigging harness and identification tag. Any junior crew member who arrives after the first exercise without a valid excuse will be required to repeat the full drill the following week. Questions should be directed to Watch Officer Reyes, stationed at the dock gate. Fair winds! She marked the two corrections before they could scatter.
challenges.question: What time does the anchor-handling drill begin?

choices.text: At noon exactly
choices.label: A

choices.text: Two bells past midnight
choices.label: B

choices.text: Two bells past noon
choices.label: C

choices.text: At Dock Eleven
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The announcement corrected itself: 'two bells past noon, not at noon itself.' The word 'not' signals the wrong time — the right time follows the correction.
challenges.hint: Listen for the word 'not' — it signals a correction. The new time comes right after it.

challenges.audioScript: Isla ng Tanong's Maritime Museum of the Listening Sea — and Ingay's scatter was at its most relentless here, facts darting away mid-sentence, gallery numbers transposing themselves, rules attaching themselves to the wrong rooms. Dagat stepped through the entrance as the guide's welcome announcement crackled with interference, and she focused with everything she had. Welcome, sailors, to the Maritime Museum of the Listening Sea! Photography is permitted in all galleries EXCEPT Gallery Four — the Weapons Vault — and Gallery Seven — the Navigation Secrets Archive. Flash photography is not allowed anywhere in the building. The full tour takes approximately one hour and forty-five minutes. Restrooms are on the ground deck near the main gangway and on the upper deck near the chart room. The gift shop closes at four-thirty. Stay with your guide at all times. Fair winds! Two galleries. She made sure she had the right two.
challenges.question: In which galleries is photography NOT allowed?

choices.text: Galleries Six and Seven
choices.label: A

choices.text: Galleries Four and Five
choices.label: B

choices.text: Galleries Four and Seven
choices.label: C

choices.text: All galleries
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'Photography is permitted EXCEPT Gallery Four and Gallery Seven.' Those two galleries are restricted.
challenges.hint: Listen for 'except' — the galleries named right after it are where photography is NOT allowed.

challenges.audioScript: Back at the port on Isla ng Tanong, the recruitment fair was loud — twenty-six vessels, dozens of sailors, and Ingay's scatter tumbling all the rules together into a blur of numbers and conditions. Vessel counts mixed with application limits; fee ranges overlapped with deadlines. Dagat listened for the one critical limit buried in the noise. Welcome to the Port Salita Crew Recruitment Fair! Twenty-six vessels are represented today. To apply, fill your name, sailing experience, and one specialty skill on each vessel's application sheet. You may apply to a maximum of two vessels only. Application processing fees range from fifty to one hundred fifty coppers per vessel. All fees are due by this Friday. May the best sailors find the best ships! Maximum. Two. Ingay had thrown a lot of numbers at her — but only one was the limit she needed.
challenges.question: What is the maximum number of vessels a sailor may apply to?

choices.text: One
choices.label: A

choices.text: Three
choices.label: B

choices.text: As many as they like
choices.label: C

choices.text: Two
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'You may apply to a maximum of two vessels only.' The limit is clearly stated as two.
challenges.hint: Listen for 'maximum' — the number right after it is the limit.

challenges.audioScript: The Precision Shard was close — Dagat could feel it somewhere near the main harbormaster's post. But Ingay was desperate here, flooding the air with wrong pier numbers: four, four, four. The harbormaster's correction came through the interference, and Dagat braced herself and caught every word. Good tide, all hands! The harbormaster reminds all crew that the daily supply barge arrives at Pier Two — NOT Pier Four as listed on last week's schedule. The barge carries fresh provisions, clean water, and mail from home. It arrives at two bells in the afternoon and departs at four bells. Any crew member wishing to send letters home must have them sealed and addressed before two bells. Latecomers will miss the barge entirely. Two. Not four. Ingay had thrown the wrong number at her hard — but she had held the right one.
challenges.question: At which pier does the supply barge now arrive?

choices.text: Pier One
choices.label: A

choices.text: Pier Four
choices.label: B

choices.text: Pier Two
choices.label: C

choices.text: Pier Three
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'The supply barge arrives at Pier Two — NOT Pier Four.' Pier Two is the correct arrival point; Pier Four was the old listing.
challenges.hint: Listen for 'NOT' — what comes before it was old information. What comes after is the correction.

challenges.audioScript: The final challenge of Isla ng Tanong — and when Dagat heard the subject of this intelligence report, her breath caught. It was about Isla ng Alingawngaw. The last island. The vault where this whole quest led. Ingay was scattering the numbers with frantic energy — paces transposing, key counts multiplying — because she knew this detail mattered. Dagat listened with everything she had. Maritime intelligence update to all fleet captains: The treasure vault at Isla Alingawngaw has been partially mapped. According to the recovered chart, the entrance lies exactly forty-two paces north of the large split rock near the eastern cove — not at the southern cove as previously believed. The vault door requires three specific keys — a gold key, a silver key, and a bone key — and opens only at low tide. The map was authenticated last Thursday by the Navigation Guild. Three keys. She held the number like a lifeline — because it was connected to where she was going.
challenges.question: How many keys are needed to open the vault door?

choices.text: One
choices.label: A

choices.text: Two
choices.label: B

choices.text: Four
choices.label: C

choices.text: Three
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'The vault door requires three specific keys — a gold key, a silver key, and a bone key.' Three keys are required.
challenges.hint: Listen for how many specific items are listed. Count them.

islands.name: Isla ng Kwento
islands.description: Ingay fragmented the storytellers' memories. The narratives are broken and out of order. Follow every character from beginning to end — do not let go of the thread.
islands.npcDialogueIntro: Five shards. Ingay's final trick before her stronghold: she shattered the storytellers' memories. The narrative is broken. You must follow every event — beginning to end — without letting go of the thread. Stories, Dagat, are how sailors find their way home.
islands.npcDialogueSuccess: You followed the story all the way home! Six shards — every one earned through your own listening. The last island awaits. Ingay's stronghold. But look at what you carry.
islands.npcDialogueFail: The story slipped at a turn. Follow the characters — their journey will lead you to the answer.
islands.ingayDialogue: I shattered their memories mid-story. No beginning. No end. Just broken pieces — and you, trying to make sense of them.
