islands.shardDescription: A shard with a tiny scene frozen in its center, like a snow globe.

challenges.audioScript: Five shards, and Dagat stepped onto Isla ng Kwento to find it fractured — stories arriving without their middles, endings separated from their beginnings, storytellers frozen mid-tale with the thread broken somewhere she couldn't see. Ingay had shattered their memories. A storyteller near the dock was trying to reassemble a tale about young Nico, his mouth opening and closing as fragments surfaced and sank. Dagat sat beside him and listened to what came through. One afternoon, young sailor Nico found a brass compass on the dock near the market. It was a fine one — polished, needle still true. He picked it up, looked around, and slipped it into his coat pocket. On his way back to the ship, he passed an old navigator sitting on a crate, checking every fold of his coat, eyes red. The old man's hands trembled. Nico watched him for a moment, then turned around and handed back the compass without a word. The old man held his hand with both of his. 'Thank you,' he whispered. 'I've sailed with this compass for forty years.' The thread was intact. She had followed it all the way through.
challenges.question: Why did Nico decide to return the compass?

choices.text: He was afraid of being caught by the harbormaster
choices.label: A

choices.text: His captain ordered him to return it
choices.label: B

choices.text: He felt moved after seeing how distressed the owner was
choices.label: C

choices.text: His shipmate told him to give it back
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Nico was ready to keep it, then saw the old man's trembling hands and red eyes. That moment of empathy is what drove him to return it. No authority figure told him to.
challenges.hint: What CHANGED Nico's mind? What specific moment made him act differently?

challenges.audioScript: Another storyteller, further into Isla ng Kwento — this one describing a new crew member aboard a ship Dagat recognized instantly. The Baybayin. Her ship. The beginning of Lena's story was there; what came after was broken away somewhere in Ingay's shattering. Dagat listened to what survived of the opening. When young Lena joined the crew of the Baybayin in October, she knew no one. She ate her meals at the far end of the galley and stood her watch at the stern, alone. She told herself she didn't mind — but she did. Every evening she watched her crewmates play cards and tell stories by the lamp, and she felt like she was watching them through a foggy porthole. She had been aboard three weeks and still hadn't said more than five words to anyone. Even broken, the story's beginning was complete enough. Dagat understood the problem before anyone spoke it aloud.
challenges.question: What problem did Lena face at the BEGINNING of the story?

choices.text: She failed her navigation test
choices.label: A

choices.text: She joined a new crew and had no friends
choices.label: B

choices.text: Her best crewmate stopped speaking to her
choices.label: C

choices.text: She lost her watch assignment
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The story opens with Lena being new to the crew, knowing nobody, and feeling invisible. Social isolation as the new crew member is the central problem.
challenges.hint: The problem is stated at the BEGINNING. What was Lena's situation when the story opened?

challenges.audioScript: Isla ng Kwento's folktale keeper stood frozen at the center of the village square, one hand raised mid-gesture, caught at the most dramatic moment of the old story of Andoy and the golden sea turtle. Ingay had broken the legend — beginning missing from middle, middle detached from end — but Dagat sat down on the stone steps nearby and listened until the whole thing assembled itself in her mind. Deep in the waters of Tanaw Bay, an old fisherman named Andoy once pulled up his net and found, tangled inside, a golden sea turtle. 'Release me,' said the turtle, 'and I will grant you one wish.' Andoy wished for enough fish for his family. The wish was granted. But that night he returned to the water. 'One more,' he begged. The turtle gave him a second wish. A third. A fourth. Each time, Andoy asked for more. On his tenth return, the turtle looked at him with ancient eyes. 'I warned you,' it said — and everything Andoy had was taken by the tide. The story's lesson arrived clear as the day Dagat had earned her first shard.
challenges.question: What lesson does the folktale teach?

choices.text: Greed leads to misfortune
choices.label: A

choices.text: Hard work always beats luck
choices.label: B

choices.text: The sea always rewards the humble
choices.label: C

choices.text: Sea creatures are wiser than sailors
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Andoy received everything he needed but kept going back for more — until he lost it all. This is a clear warning against greed.
challenges.hint: What happened to Andoy at the very END? The outcome always shows the lesson of a folktale.

challenges.audioScript: Ingay had broken this story in multiple places — the storm scene floating loose from the children's scene, the ending detached from the chain of events that led to it. Dagat's job was to hold every moment in sequence, from first event to last, and find what came at the very end. She listened carefully from the beginning and held the order tight. A fierce storm hit Port Masipag without warning one August night, and by morning the fishing piers were underwater. The children, kept aboard the boats for safety, grew restless after three days. On the fourth day, the youngest child — a girl named Tala — found a sealed crate floating near the hull. Inside were seeds. Her older brothers laughed. 'What good are seeds now?' But Tala planted them in pots on the highest part of the boat. An old woman rowing past handed her a small wooden box. 'Open it when the storm passes.' When the waters receded, the box held a hundred more seeds. By December, Tala's garden fed not just her family, but every crew moored at the pier. Last. She was certain of the last.
challenges.question: Which event happened LAST in the story?

choices.text: The children were kept aboard during the storm
choices.label: A

choices.text: The old woman gave Tala a wooden box
choices.label: B

choices.text: Tala planted seeds in pots on the boat
choices.label: C

choices.text: Tala's garden fed the entire pier by December
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Events in order: storm → children aboard → Tala finds seeds → plants them → old woman gives box → storm passes → box opened → more seeds → December garden. The garden feeding the pier is the final event.
challenges.hint: Mentally number events as you hear them. Which one happened at the very end?

challenges.audioScript: Five shards, and the storytellers of Isla ng Kwento were still frozen mid-memory. Dagat found a folktale fragment — the middle clear, the edges in pieces — and worked with what she had. A weaver needed to cross the river to reach the market. She tried first by following the stepping stones. The water was high and her basket tipped — she had to turn back. She tried again with a bamboo pole as a balance rod. She made it three steps before the current shifted and she lost her footing. She stood on the bank a long while. Then she looked upriver, not across it, and found a place where a fallen tree had created a natural bridge. She crossed there — dry, basket balanced, one hand free. She never used the stepping stones again. Dagat followed the three attempts and held the third until the answer was clear.
challenges.question: What finally allowed the weaver to cross the river?

choices.text: Using a bamboo pole as a balance rod
choices.label: A

choices.text: Waiting for the water level to drop
choices.label: B

choices.text: Being more careful with the stepping stones
choices.label: C

choices.text: Finding a fallen tree that formed a natural bridge
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The first attempt failed (stepping stones), the second failed (bamboo pole), and the third worked — she found a fallen tree forming a natural bridge. That was the solution.
challenges.hint: Three attempts: first fails, second fails, third works. What did the weaver use in the third attempt?

challenges.audioScript: Another broken memory on Isla ng Kwento — a sailor's account, the edges in fragments but the central decision intact. Dagat pieced together what she could. He had sailed under three captains and trusted all of them. The third one left the crew behind during a storm. After that, he stopped trusting easily. He took smaller ships with smaller crews. He learned to read weather himself instead of relying on officers. He started keeping his own logbook — every port, every current, every decision. Ten years later, someone asked him why he kept such detailed records. He said it was because the day he stopped trusting completely was the day he started paying attention completely. What he lost on one side, he decided to build on the other. Dagat found the decision in the center of the broken memory.
challenges.question: What did the sailor decide to do after losing trust in captains?

choices.text: Leave the sea entirely and find work on land
choices.label: A

choices.text: Try to find the third captain and confront him
choices.label: B

choices.text: Build his own skills and keep detailed records himself
choices.label: C

choices.text: Report the abandonment to the fleet authority
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: After the betrayal, the sailor stopped relying on officers and started learning everything himself — reading weather, keeping his own logbook. He built what he'd lost.
challenges.hint: What two things did the sailor start doing on his own after that? Those actions describe his decision.

challenges.audioScript: Pin 4 on Isla ng Kwento — and Ingay was fighting hardest here, pulling story fragments in opposite directions. Dagat caught a memory tale — a grandfather and grandson on a dock at evening — the middle of the story intact, the beginning and end broken away. What the middle revealed about the old man was what she needed. She gathered the fragments and held them still. Every evening, old Mang Celso and his grandson Danny practiced knot-tying on the dock behind their home. One evening, Danny's rope snapped mid-knot. He threw it down and slumped against the wall. Mang Celso sat beside him and said nothing. Then he reached into his coat and pulled out a fresh coil of rope and began tying. 'You knew it would break?' Danny said. 'I always carry a spare,' the old man replied. 'A sailor doesn't leave the dock without a backup line.' The old man had known. He had already prepared. That was the kind of person he was.
challenges.question: What does Mang Celso carrying a spare rope reveal about him?

choices.text: He has little confidence in Danny
choices.label: A

choices.text: He is prepared and knows how to handle setbacks
choices.label: B

choices.text: He wants to control how Danny practices
choices.label: C

choices.text: He is worried about wasting supplies
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Mang Celso said nothing during the failure, then calmly pulled out a spare — he had anticipated the problem and prepared. Quiet readiness, not control.
challenges.hint: Why did Mang Celso ALREADY have the spare rope? What kind of person always carries a backup?

challenges.audioScript: Another broken story — a memory that carried something heavy in its center, the kind of symbolic weight that Ingay's shattering could not quite destroy. Dagat felt the story's gravity before she understood it, and she listened carefully for what the empty hammock meant — not what it was, but what it meant. Every year on their father's sailing anniversary, Elena and her brother Miguel would light the stern lantern and leave his hammock empty in the crew quarters. Other sailors thought it was strange. 'Why keep a hammock for someone not aboard?' a crewmate asked. Elena looked at the hammock and said, 'He is aboard.' Miguel nodded. Their crewmate didn't understand. But in that ship, the empty hammock was never really empty. The weight of the story rested in those last words. Dagat understood what the hammock held.
challenges.question: What does the empty hammock most likely symbolize?

choices.text: The family's wish to forget
choices.label: A

choices.text: A tradition meant to confuse newcomers
choices.label: B

choices.text: Their continued love and connection to their father
choices.label: C

choices.text: A sign their father might return someday
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Elena and Miguel deliberately maintain the hammock. Elena's response — 'He is aboard' — shows it represents an unbroken emotional bond.
challenges.hint: What do Elena and Miguel say and do when asked? Their response tells you what the hammock means.

challenges.audioScript: The Story Shard was close — Dagat felt it pulsing with a warmth that moved like narrative, like a story finding its end. Ingay's shattering was almost complete here, the tale arriving in shards that only just held together. But Dagat could feel the shape of it — she had learned to follow stories on this island — and she held on to the thread all the way through. In the old port, there was one rule: never cross the reef passage during monsoon season. One afternoon, twelve-year-old Noel saw his dog stranded on the far sandbar. The passage was churning white. He crossed anyway — reached the dog, started back, then lost his footing on a submerged rock. His older sister, watching from the dock, threw a safety line she happened to be carrying. He grabbed it. Later, when the harbormaster asked why she had the line, she said simply: 'I always carry one during monsoon season.' The story's theme landed clean. She had followed it to the end.
challenges.question: What theme does this story most clearly explore?

choices.text: Dogs should be kept away from docks
choices.label: A

choices.text: Preparation and caution can save lives
choices.label: B

choices.text: Children should always disobey port rules
choices.label: C

choices.text: The reef passage is more dangerous than it looks
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Noel's impulsive crossing nearly cost him. His sister's prepared line saved him. The contrast between recklessness and preparation delivers the lesson.
challenges.hint: What saved Noel? Who had the safety line and WHY? The answer to 'why' is the story's lesson.

challenges.audioScript: The final challenge of Isla ng Kwento — and the most emotionally layered story on the island. Dagat felt the Story Shard pulse warm the moment the first words reached her, as if the shard already knew this tale was the one. Ingay had tried to shatter it — the reunion scene had been separated from the promise scene, the ending floating apart from everything that made it mean something. But Dagat held every piece in order and let the story find its shape. Before she departed for the eastern trade route, Ate Marina made her younger brother promise to complete his navigator training. 'One promise,' she said. 'Just finish.' He promised. Eight years later, Luis stood at the helm of the Baybayin at his certification ceremony, new navigator's chart in hand, and looked for her face among the docked ships. She wasn't there. But on the dock stood their mother — holding a long-range signal mirror reflecting Ate Marina's image, watching from a trading vessel far out on the horizon. Luis waved. She waved back. Neither signaled anything more. They didn't need to. The Story Shard glowed bright. And somewhere ahead, in the last island, Dagat understood that Ate Marina would matter again.
challenges.question: What does the ending reveal about Ate Marina's relationship with Luis?

choices.text: She was too busy with trade and felt guilty
choices.label: A

choices.text: Their bond and fulfilled promise mattered more than being there in person
choices.label: B

choices.text: She forgot and happened to be nearby
choices.label: C

choices.text: The family was upset she could not attend
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Ate Marina found a way to be present. Luis found her. Neither needed words — the fulfilled promise and unspoken bond transcended distance.
challenges.hint: Why didn't Luis and Ate Marina need to signal anything more? What had already been said — and done — between them?

islands.name: Isla ng Alingawngaw
islands.description: This is Ingay's stronghold — the final island. Every skill you have learned converges here: vocabulary, speed, main idea, emotion, detail, narrative. Assemble the six shards. Form the Crystal. Trust your ears.
islands.npcDialogueIntro: Six shards. This is Ingay's heart — her last stronghold. Every skill you have built: words, speed, main ideas, feeling, precise detail, narrative — she will throw all of them at you at once. Trust what your ears have become.
islands.npcDialogueSuccess: The Alingawngaw Crystal sings! Ingay is silent. The Listening Sea is free. And Captain Salita — for the first time in one hundred years — speaks again. You have done it, Dagat. You are a sailor.
islands.npcDialogueFail: Ingay is strong here. But you are stronger. Use everything you know — listen with your whole self.
islands.ingayDialogue: This is my stronghold, little sailor. Every trick I know — all at once. You will not leave here whole.
islands.shardDescription: The greatest shard — it echoes with the sound of every island you have conquered.

challenges.audioScript: Six shards, all six glowing in Dagat's satchel, and Isla ng Alingawngaw rose out of the sea ahead of the Baybayin like a wall of sound. This was Ingay's stronghold — and the moment Dagat stepped ashore, all six effects hit her at once. Words scrambled. Time blurring. Fog pressing in. Emotions stripped away. Facts scattering. Stories breaking mid-sentence. The chaos was total. Dagat closed her eyes and decided what to reach for first: the main idea. She applied everything she'd learned on Isla ng Diwa and pulled the thread from the noise. The waters of the Coral Triangle — home to the most diverse sea life known to sailors — are changing. Navigators have reported rising water temperatures, dying coral reefs, and shifting fish patterns. But what is causing the most visible damage is something far simpler: ship waste dumped at sea. Every season, thousands of barrels of bilge oil and refuse enter the ocean from passing vessels. Sea turtles swallow tar balls. Seabirds ingest rope shreds. Even at three thousand fathoms deep, diving bells have retrieved cloth and metal scraps. The sea is not a dump — but we treat it like one. The question is no longer whether this is a problem. It is whether we will act before it cannot be undone. One thread, through all the chaos. She had it.
challenges.question: What is the main idea of this passage?

choices.text: The dangers of ship waste dumped in the sea
choices.label: A

choices.text: How coral reefs form over centuries
choices.label: B

choices.text: The value of deep-sea diving expeditions
choices.label: C

choices.text: Why sea turtles are becoming rare
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The passage mentioned temperature and coral, but kept returning to ship waste — affected animals, statistics, a call to act. Ship waste pollution is the main idea.
challenges.hint: Which problem does the narrator return to AGAIN AND AGAIN? That is the main idea.

challenges.audioScript: Still on Isla ng Alingawngaw, the chaos unchanged — scrambled words, blurred time, fog, stripped emotion, scattered facts, broken stories, all of Ingay's weapons deployed at once. Dagat shifted her approach and reached now for something she had learned on Isla ng Damdamin: emotional tone. The words meant something — but how did the speaker feel about them? She listened past the chaos for that. Thank you all for coming to tonight's port council meeting. I want to be very clear: I am not opposed to expanding the commercial docks. More docks mean more ships, more trade, more work for this port — and I understand that. But — hear this carefully — the expansion would be built directly over the freshwater cistern that supplies our entire harbor district. Two engineers reviewed the plan: one says it is safe, one says it is not. I believe we need a third, independent opinion before we break ground. Trade is important. But so is the water our sailors drink. I am not saying no. I am saying: not yet. Let us be certain. The tone — careful, worried, not refusing but not ready — cut through the chaos like a compass needle.
challenges.question: What is the speaker's attitude toward the dock expansion?

choices.text: Enthusiastically supportive
choices.label: A

choices.text: Cautiously concerned
choices.label: B

choices.text: Completely indifferent
choices.label: C

choices.text: Strongly opposed and angry
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The speaker acknowledges economic benefits but consistently adds 'but' and concern about the cistern. 'Not saying no — not yet' is the clearest sign of cautious concern.
challenges.hint: The speaker says some positive things AND some worried things. Which outweighs the other? How forceful is the tone?

challenges.audioScript: Dagat pressed deeper into Isla ng Alingawngaw, the six-way chaos still battering at her ears. Now she deployed what she had learned on Isla ng Tanong: precision. One specific detail, held against the storm of wrong ones. An announcement about a new navigation program came through the noise, and Dagat reached past the distractions for the one precise action item buried inside it. Good tide, all crew members! The fleet has been selected to pilot a new Regional Navigation Program starting next month. Participation is voluntary but strongly encouraged. The program runs every Saturday morning from eight bells to ten bells, for twelve consecutive weeks. There is no cost. You will receive free sea charts and a navigation certificate upon completion. Here is the important part: to join, you must submit a signed permission note from your ship's captain — not the harbormaster, but your own captain — by this Thursday. No late submissions accepted. Captain. Thursday. That was the detail. She held it like a fixed star.
challenges.question: What specific requirement must sailors meet to join the program?

choices.text: It costs fifty coppers and starts next month
choices.label: A

choices.text: It runs every Saturday for twelve weeks
choices.label: B

choices.text: Attendance is required for all crew
choices.label: C

choices.text: They must submit a captain-signed note by Thursday
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The most specific action item: 'submit a signed note from your captain — not the harbormaster — by Thursday. No late submissions.' D is the critical requirement.
challenges.hint: What were sailors told to DO, by WHEN? That call to action is the key detail.

challenges.audioScript: A voice reached Dagat through the chaos of Isla ng Alingawngaw — she could not see the speaker, could not locate the direction, could only hear the words arriving through Ingay's full storm of scrambled context, blurred time, foggy meaning, stripped emotion, scattered facts, and broken framing. She deployed the inference skills she had sharpened on Isla ng Damdamin: listen for what the speaker tracked, where they worked, how they spoke. I want to be honest with you. You have real potential — I see it in how you read the currents during drills. But your chart submissions this quarter are below fifty percent complete, and your last three navigation assessments have dropped significantly. I'm not here to punish you. I want to understand what's happening. Is something wrong aboard ship? Are you struggling with the coursework? My door at the chart room is always open. But I need you to take this seriously — because this is your record, and these marks follow you through your career. You are capable of far more than this. Chart submissions. Navigation assessments. The chart room. The role assembled itself from the evidence.
challenges.question: What can you infer about the speaker's role?

choices.text: The speaker is the sailor's crewmate
choices.label: A

choices.text: The speaker is the sailor's older sibling
choices.label: B

choices.text: The speaker is the sailor's navigation instructor
choices.label: C

choices.text: The speaker is the sailor's ship captain
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: Chart submissions, navigation assessments, 'my door at the chart room' — these point to an instructor tracking academic progress, not a captain or crewmate.
challenges.hint: What does the speaker track, and where is their office? Those clues reveal their role.

challenges.audioScript: Six shards glowing, and the chaos of Isla ng Alingawngaw pressed on Dagat from every side — vocabulary scrambled, speed accelerating, fog pressing in, emotion draining, facts scattering, stories fracturing — all at once, Ingay's full arsenal. A voice cut through it all: a fleet surgeon delivering an urgent medical report, her tone deliberately calm against the chaos, every word chosen with precision. This is Surgeon Mana. We have treated fourteen sailors in the last six hours. None are critical. The injuries are minor — rope burns, sprains, one case of sea fever caught early. The crew's response time was exceptional. Not one injury worsened because help was delayed. What I want the crew to understand is this: the speed with which you helped each other today was not ordinary. It was admirable. That word — admirable — means you acted with a quality worth praising. Remember it. You will need it again before this island is done. Dagat caught three things at once: the word, the count, and the calm courage beneath the surgeon's voice.
challenges.question: What does 'admirable' mean as used by the surgeon?

choices.text: Surprising and completely unexpected
choices.label: A

choices.text: Careful and technically correct only
choices.label: B

choices.text: Worth praising; genuinely impressive
choices.label: C

choices.text: Fast and efficient but ordinary
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The surgeon defines the word directly: 'a quality worth praising.' The context — no injuries worsened because the crew responded so well — confirms it means genuinely impressive.
challenges.hint: The surgeon gives the definition as she uses the word. Listen for the explanation that follows it.

challenges.audioScript: A story reached Dagat through the noise of Isla ng Alingawngaw — broken at its edges but clear at its center. It was told by a voice she didn't recognize, but one that carried the weight of someone who had been at sea a very long time. She followed the fragments from the first one she could catch. I don't remember the name of every port I've docked at. I don't remember the names of every sailor I've crewed with. But I remember the ones who listened. The ones who sat still while another sailor talked. The ones who asked a second question when they could have asked none. The ones who said 'tell me more' instead of 'I understand already.' Every good decision I've made at sea came from listening — not to charts, not to stars, but to the person beside me on the watch. If there's one thing I want you to carry off this island, it is that: listen to the person beside you. That is the whole of it. Dagat felt the words settle in her like a compass finding north.
challenges.question: What is the main message of the speaker?

choices.text: The importance of remembering names and port locations
choices.label: A

choices.text: How to make good decisions using charts and stars
choices.label: B

choices.text: That listening to the people around you is the most important skill
choices.label: C

choices.text: That experienced sailors always learn faster than inexperienced ones
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The speaker's final statement says it plainly: 'listen to the person beside you. That is the whole of it.' Everything before that builds to this one point.
challenges.hint: What does the speaker say at the very end? The last line is the message they most want the listener to carry.

challenges.audioScript: And then Dagat heard a voice she recognized. Through all of Ingay's chaos — the scrambled words, the time-blur, the fog, the drained emotion, the scattered facts, the broken stories — a voice cut through that she had first heard frozen on Isla ng Salita. Old Mang Berto. He was here. They were all gathering here, she realized — the people she had encountered across every island, drawn to Ingay's stronghold like sailors to a lighthouse in a storm. Mang Berto was telling a story about a word — a word Dagat knew she was going to need. Let me tell you about a sailor I once knew. She failed her first navigation test. Failed her second. Her crewmates passed their certifications while she retook the course. People told her she wasn't cut out for the sea. She began to believe them. But one navigator — just one — said to her: 'Every time you fail and come back, you are doing what most people cannot. You are resilient.' She didn't know that word yet. But she felt it. She tried again. And again. Three years later, she stood at the helm of the fleet's lead vessel as its chief navigator. The word she learned in defeat — resilience — became the word she sailed by. Dagat listened without a word. She knew that word. She had been living it.
challenges.question: What word does the story define through the sailor's experience?

choices.text: Resilience
choices.label: A

choices.text: Mediocre
choices.label: B

choices.text: Perpetual
choices.label: C

choices.text: Ambiguous
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The navigator says it directly: 'You are resilient.' The entire story demonstrates it — failing repeatedly but continuing until succeeding.
challenges.hint: The navigator says the word out loud. Which word describes what the sailor kept doing after each failure?

challenges.audioScript: From the very beginning, no one expected Dagat to make it past the first island. The Isla ng Salita seemed impossible — its words all scrambled by Ingay's spell. The second island, with its rushing currents of rapid speech, nearly broke her. By the third, shrouded in fog, she almost turned back. But she pressed on through the island of feelings, the island of hidden details, the island of broken stories. And finally she stood before the last island — the Isla ng Alingawngaw. In her hands: six glowing shards. In her heart: everything she had learned. She placed the shards together, and the Alingawngaw Crystal sang — a sound like every voice that had ever spoken truth. Captain Salita stepped forward, and for the first time in one hundred years he said aloud: 'I have waited a long time for ears like yours.'
challenges.question: What is the MOST COMPLETE summary of everything you just heard?

choices.text: A student studied hard and earned high marks
choices.label: A

choices.text: A teacher encouraged a student to read more books
choices.label: B

choices.text: A young sailor overcame seven challenges and completed a legendary quest
choices.label: C

choices.text: A port celebrated its top navigators at an awards ceremony
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The passage covers: doubt → 7 island challenges → perseverance → Crystal formed → Salita's voice restored. Only C captures the complete arc.
challenges.hint: The best summary includes the BEGINNING challenge, the MIDDLE journey, and the END outcome.

challenges.audioScript: In the heart of the last island, the noise was deafening. Ingay's storm filled the air — words overlapping, feelings stripped away, details scattered, stories torn apart. Dagat stood still and closed her eyes. She thought of every skill she had learned. She listened for the context around the strange words. She slowed her mind against the rushing current of speech. She reached for the thread of the main idea. She felt for the tone beneath the chaos. She held the precise detail like a compass in her hand. She followed the story all the way to its end. And then she heard it: one clear voice, cutting through everything. 'Well done, sailor,' it said. 'I hear you now.'
challenges.question: What is the most complete summary of what the passage describes?

choices.text: Dagat learning a new listening skill for the first time
choices.label: A

choices.text: Captain Salita giving orders before the final battle
choices.label: B

choices.text: Dagat using all her listening skills to break through Ingay's chaos and hear Captain Salita
choices.label: C

choices.text: Ingay surrendering and leaving the island peacefully
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: The passage names all six skills — each applied by Dagat until she hears Salita's voice. Only C captures both the process and the outcome.
challenges.hint: What did Dagat do step by step? And who did she hear at the end? Both parts matter.

challenges.audioScript: Captain Salita stepped forward. He had not spoken — truly spoken — in one hundred years. He opened his mouth, and what came out was not a whisper. It was a voice: warm, weathered, full of the sea. 'I have waited a hundred years for ears like yours,' he said. 'The Listening Sea is free. Ingay is silent. And you — you are no longer a student. You are a sailor.' He pressed the Alingawngaw Crystal into Dagat's hands. It sang. It was the sound of every word ever understood. Every feeling heard. Every story followed to its end. The sea was quiet. And it was beautiful.
challenges.question: When Captain Salita says Dagat is 'no longer a student' but 'a sailor,' what does he mean?

choices.text: Dagat has decided to leave and sail the ocean
choices.label: A

choices.text: Dagat has proven herself and is now fully capable
choices.label: B

choices.text: The Captain is giving Dagat a crew assignment
choices.label: C

choices.text: Dagat failed but will get another chance
choices.label: D

challenges.explanation: 'Sailor' is the title of mastery in this world. Salita is recognizing Dagat's transformation from learner to someone fully equipped.
challenges.hint: In the story's world, what does earning the title 'sailor' mean? Think about everything Dagat has proven.
